# Transcrição: 01-Rabbi Story

- **Fonte:** 01-Rabbi Story
- **Duração:** 09:01
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

I went to the woods with 13 rabbis. I'm not Jewish, I'm a storyteller. The rabbis were sent with me to the woods because they were deemed by their congregations as being boring, not connecting with the people who they needed to connect with, which is to say they were annoyed. They were not excited about being deemed boring, and so when they arrived at our little hut for the weekend, none of them were pleased. I stood in front of them and I began telling them about storytelling. I explained to them that their problem was they weren't connecting with their congregations because they weren't sharing of themselves. They weren't talking about themselves. They weren't subtly sharing their lives with their congregants. And they fought me tooth and nail. They told me it's about the text. They told me we're here for the text. We teach the text and I continually told them nobody cares about your text. The last thing they come to Temple for is the text. In fact, in a Jewish Temple, the text remains rolled up and in a container most of the time. You don't even put it out for people to read. It can't be about the text and yet they insisted it was the text. I fought with them on Friday night. I fought with them on Saturday. Saturday night we had a campfire by the lake. We all sat around a large fire and miraculously two bottles of whiskey appeared. And they began to get passed around the circle. And as they began to get passed around the circle, the rabbis started to loosen up a little. I asked someone to tell a story. And one of the rabbis told a story about a funeral gone bad. A funeral where he had accidentally buried someone with a ring that was supposed to have been removed. And so in the middle of the night, the rabbi with a friend dug the person up, removed the ring, and put the body back in the ground. And then another rabbi told a story. In the Jewish custom, you can't destroy a book that has the word God in the book, which means rabbis end up with piles of books that they don't know what to do with. And so one of the rabbis explained that they offer a discount on burial if they're allowed to line the interior of the coffin with books they no longer want or need. They don't do it during the service. It's after the service he explained. We went around the circle and every rabbi had at least one crazy funeral story to tell. The alcohol worked on them. The spirit of storytelling worked on them. By the end of the night, we were laughing. We were crying. It was a wonderful night of storytelling around the fire.

## [03:03 - 06:06]

The next morning after breakfast, we gathered to talk again. Sorry. The next morning we gathered in our hall to have our conversation again, and I told this to the rabbis. I said that my wife and children are Jewish and I am not. And every time I walk into a temple, I feel out of place. I wonder if I'm even wanted. I know that in the Jewish custom, it is often preferred that one Jewish person marry another Jewish person. And in some communities, it's actually disparaged when a Jewish person marries outside the faith. And so I never feel truly connected to the places of worship that my children and my wife go to. I always feel like an outsider. I told the rabbis that sitting around that campfire that night, drinking whiskey and listening to them tell stories, I told them I'd never felt so close to the Jewish religion before. It never felt so accessible to me before. It never felt so normal and ordinary and real world as it did that night listening to those rabbis tell stories. I explained to them that if I had gone to a temple and sat down and I had heard a rabbi tell a story, maybe not about a funeral gone bad, maybe not with a bottle of whiskey in their hand. But if I had heard a rabbi in one of these temples tell a story about themselves, make an attempt to connect to me as a person, share something vulnerable, honest, authentic with their congregation, I suspect I would have felt more welcome in all of these places. I think that the distance that I feel from my wife's religion and my children's religion is the distance that storytelling could bridge. The traditions and the customs of my wife and my children are never going to be mine. And I'm never going to feel as close to them as I feel to the customs and traditions of my own upbringing. But I could get closer. I could get closer through storytelling. If the person who is running the place, the rabbi in charge, would reach out a little bit and let me know that he's a Red Sox fan, that he owns a dog, that he's afraid of the dark, that one time when he was 16 years old he crashed his father's car into a tree, that when he was 23 years old he stole a pack of gum from the department store and still feels bad about it to this day. If he would share stories like that, be a little vulnerable, be a little honest with me and his congregants, I feel like we'd be a lot closer.

## [06:06 - 09:01]

That temple would feel like a more welcoming place for me. I tell you that story because I think it applies to just about everything we do. If you're a business owner reaching out to your customers, your clients, your investors, being a little more vulnerable and authentic, I think it'll bring you closer together. If you're a teacher standing in front of a class and you're willing to share of yourself, talk about your life, you'll be a little closer. Most of the teachers who I grew up with, I couldn't have told you if they were married or single, I couldn't have told you if they were a pet owner, I couldn't have told you if they were parents. They were the teacher and I was the student and the space between us was enormous. It would have taken so little to bridge that space. No matter what you do, you can be closer to people. You can connect with them. You can get them to trust you and believe in you. It just takes a story. If you can craft that story a little better, if you can use some of the strategies and skills I've talked about, maybe that bridge can be complete. Maybe you can reach out and that person will take your hand. Maybe you'll find the trust and the belief and the friendship and the love, all of the things you're looking for just by sharing a story. I walk into temples with my children these days. I feel a little more connected to the place than I'm entering. I feel a little more welcome when I walk into a temple. And it was because of a night I spent around a fire with whiskey and rabbis. Just imagine how much more welcome and happy I would be if that happened every time. If every time I walked into one of those spaces, someone shared something with me, a story or a little bit of themselves to better connect with me. We tell stories because we want people to be closer to us in every context of our lives. It's time to tell stories. That story you've been working on, that personal story, that story you've been working on, that professional story, it's time to get it into shape. Speak it out loud. Prepare it and then go forth and tell it. Tell it to the masses and get closer to the people who you're striving to get closer to. Get them to believe you and trust you so that you can accomplish all the things you're hoping to accomplish.
